content:
# Leviticus Chapter 19 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 69r–69v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_019_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_019_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 105–106

---

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**2** "Speak to the entire congregation of the sons of Israel and say to them: You shall be holy, for I the LORD your God am holy.

**3** Every one of you shall revere his mother and his father, and you shall keep my Sabbaths. I am the LORD your God.

**4** Do not turn to idols or make for yourselves cast images of gods. I am the LORD your God.

**5** "When you offer a sacrifice of peace offerings to the LORD, you shall offer it so that you may be accepted.

**6** It shall be eaten on the day of your sacrifice, and on the next day; and anything left over until the third day shall be burned with fire.

**7** If it is eaten at all on the third day, it is an offensive thing; it will not be accepted.

**8** And everyone who eats it shall bear his iniquity, because he has profaned what is holy to the LORD, and that person shall be cut off from his people.

**9** "When you reap the harvest of your land, you shall not reap your field right to its edge, nor shall you gather the gleanings of your harvest.

**10** And you shall not strip your vineyard bare, nor shall you gather the fallen grapes of your vineyard. You shall leave them for the poor and for the sojourner. I am the LORD your God.

**11** "You shall not steal; you shall not deal falsely; you shall not lie to one another.

**12** You shall not swear by my name falsely, and so profane the name of your God. I am the LORD.

**13** You shall not oppress your neighbor or rob him. The wages of a hired worker shall not remain with you all night until the morning.

**14** You shall not curse the deaf or put a stumbling block before the blind, but you shall fear your God. I am the LORD.

**15** "You shall do no injustice in judgment. You shall not be partial to the poor or defer to the great, but in righteousness shall you judge your neighbor.

**16** You shall not go around as a slanderer among your people, and you shall not stand against the blood of your neighbor. I am the LORD.

**17** "You shall not hate your brother in your heart. You shall surely reprove your neighbor, lest you incur sin because of him.

**18** You shall not take vengeance or bear a grudge against the sons of your own people, but you shall love your neighbor as yourself. I am the LORD.

**19** "You shall keep my statutes. You shall not let your cattle breed with a different kind. You shall not sow your field with two kinds of seed, nor shall you wear a garment made of two kinds of material mixed together.

**20** "If a man lies sexually with a woman who is a slave, designated for another man but not yet ransomed or given her freedom, an inquiry shall be held. They shall not be put to death, because she was not free.

**21** But he shall bring his guilt offering to the LORD, to the entrance of the tent of meeting — a ram as a guilt offering.

**22** And the priest shall make atonement for him with the ram of the guilt offering before the LORD for the sin that he has committed, and he shall be forgiven for the sin that he has committed.

**23** "When you come into the land and plant any kind of tree for food, you shall count its fruit as forbidden. Three years it shall be forbidden to you; it shall not be eaten.

**24** And in the fourth year all its fruit shall be holy, an offering of praise to the LORD.

**25** But in the fifth year you may eat of its fruit, to increase its yield for you. I am the LORD your God.

**26** "You shall not eat anything with the blood. You shall not interpret omens or tell fortunes.

**27** You shall not round off the hair on your temples or mar the edges of your beard.

**28** You shall not make any cuts on your body for the dead or tattoo yourselves. I am the LORD.

**29** "Do not profane your daughter by making her a prostitute, lest the land fall into prostitution and the land become full of depravity.

**30** You shall keep my Sabbaths and revere my sanctuary. I am the LORD.

**31** "Do not turn to mediums or to necromancers; do not seek them out, and so make yourselves unclean by them. I am the LORD your God.

**32** "You shall stand up before the gray head and honor the face of an old man, and you shall fear your God. I am the LORD.

**33** "When a sojourner sojourns with you in your land, you shall not wrong him.

**34** You shall treat the sojourner who sojourns with you as the native among you, and you shall love him as yourself, for you were sojourners in the land of Egypt. I am the LORD your God.

**35** "You shall do no wrong in judgment, in measures of length or weight or quantity.

**36** You shall have just balances, just weights, a just ephah, and a just hin. I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt.

**37** And you shall observe all my statutes and all my rules, and do them. I am the LORD."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 69r–69v:**
Leviticus 19 occupies the latter portion of folio 69r and the opening of folio 69v in the Leningrad Codex, following immediately upon the boundary legislation of chapter 18. Within the classic source-critical scheme, chapter 19 is assigned to the Holiness Code (H source, chs. 17–26) and is widely regarded as its ethical and theological heart. The chapter is structured as a series of apodictic commands, mostly second-person plural, framed by the foundational summons to holiness (v. 2) and the concluding call to full observance (v. 37). The Masoretic text is well-preserved in B19A with no significant lacunae. The chapter is notable for its deliberate echo of the Decalogue — the prohibitions on theft (v. 11), false swearing by God's name (v. 12), honoring parents (v. 3), Sabbath observance (v. 3), no idols (v. 4), and murder-adjacent commands (v. 16) recapitulate and develop the Ten Commandments in social and ethical application.

The chapter is unified by the recurring formula "I am the LORD" / "I am the LORD your God," which appears fifteen times (vv. 3, 4, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 25, 26 [implied], 28, 30, 31, 32, 34, 36, 37). This extraordinary density of the self-identification formula makes chapter 19 the most YHWH-formula-intensive chapter in all of Leviticus, giving each individual command its grounding in the divine character and covenant identity. The commands are not a legal code in the abstract; they are extensions of YHWH's own holiness into the social fabric of Israel.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 105–106:**
Leviticus 19 in the Vaticanus spans pages 105 and 106. Page 105 (imaged as `LEV_019_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`) carries the beginning of the chapter through the agricultural laws and social ethics, while page 106 (imaged as `LEV_019_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`) continues through the holiness regulations to the chapter's end. The LXX of chapter 19 is largely equivalent to the MT in content, structure, and sequence. The LXX rendering of the opening command (v. 2: "Be holy, for I am holy") is precise and famous: Ἅγιοι ἔσεσθε, ὅτι ἐγὼ ἅγιός εἰμι κύριος ὁ θεὸς ὑμῶν. The LXX adds κύριος ὁ θεὸς ὑμῶν ("the LORD your God") to the holiness command, slightly expanding the MT. Several LXX variants in this chapter are exegetically significant, particularly at vv. 18, 20, 23–24, and 36.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. "The entire congregation" (v. 2) — kol adat:**
The opening command in v. 2 is unique in Leviticus: Moses is directed to speak not to Aaron's sons or the priests but to kol adat bney Yisra'el — "the entire congregation of the sons of Israel." The word adat (from eda, "congregation, assembly, community") denotes the assembled covenant community as a deliberate body. The term is rendered "entire congregation" rather than "whole assembly" (NIV) or "whole congregation" (ESV) — both are acceptable; "congregation" for eda is the standard rendering in Leviticus. The address to the entire people is deliberate: holiness in chapter 19 is not a priestly prerogative or a cultic specialization — it is the calling of the whole people of God.

**2. "You shall be holy, for I the LORD your God am holy" (v. 2) — qedoshim tihyu ki qadosh ani YHWH eloheikhem:**
This verse is the theological summit of the Holiness Code and one of the most important sentences in the entire Pentateuch. The command qedoshim tihyu (plural imperative of qadash, "to be holy, to be set apart, consecrated") is grounded in the divine character: ki qadosh ani YHWH eloheikhem ("for holy am I — the LORD your God"). The holiness demanded of Israel is not a human achievement but an imitation and participation in the divine nature. The structure of the Hebrew places qadosh ("holy") in emphatic predicate position before the subject — "for holy am I." The rendering "for I the LORD your God am holy" maintains the emphasis: YHWH's holiness is the ground and model for Israel's holiness. This verse is the fountainhead of the NT imitatio Dei ethic (cf. Matt 5:48; 1 Pet 1:15–16, which directly cites this verse).

**3. "Revere" (v. 3) — tira'u:**
The verb yare (Qal, "to fear, revere, stand in awe of") is rendered "revere" in v. 3: "Every one of you shall revere his mother and his father." The command appears with parents as its object, rather than the more typical "honor" (kaved, as in Exod 20:12). The choice of yare (fear/revere) rather than kaved (honor/give weight to) is significant: it suggests the deep relational posture of awe and reverence, not merely external observance or deference. "Revere" is preferred over "fear" (which in modern English connotes fright) and over "honor" (which is the Decalogue's word — a different term). Notably, the order in Leviticus 19:3 is mother before father — the reverse of the Decalogue (Exod 20:12: "Honor your father and your mother"). Rabbinic tradition treats this reversal as deliberate: one might be more likely to honor the father (socially dominant figure) than the mother, so the Torah reverses the order to emphasize that the mother is equally to be revered. The same verse immediately conjoins parental reverence with Sabbath observance — the family and the sacred time are placed side by side as the two fundamental structures of Israelite holiness.

**4. "Idols" (v. 4) — ha-elilim:**
The term elilim (singular elil) is a deliberately contemptuous word for false gods, likely related to the root al ("nothing, worthlessness") — a diminutive or pejorative of El ("God"). It connotes "nothings, worthless things, non-entities." The rendering "idols" is standard (ESV, NASB, KJV) and accurately captures the polemical force. The same verse prohibits "cast images of gods" (massekhot elohim) — massekhah is the term for a molten/cast metal image (from nasakh, "to pour out, cast"). The phrase massekhot elohim ("molten images of gods") is rendered "cast images of gods." The combination elilim / massekhot creates a two-term polemic: both the concepts (worthless non-entities) and the objects (cast metal forms) are condemned.

**5. "Peace offerings" (v. 5) — zevach shelamim:**
The זֶבַח שְׁלָמִים (zevach shelamim) is the well-being offering or fellowship offering, the sacrificial category characterized by communal eating of the sacrificial meal. The rendering "peace offerings" follows KJV/NASB/ESV tradition and is retained here as the conventional formal-equivalent term. The root shalom ("wholeness, peace, well-being") is reflected in the name. The specific legislation in vv. 5–8 governs the time limit for eating the peace offering: day of sacrifice and the next day, but not the third day. Anything eaten on the third day is pigul (v. 7) — an "offensive thing," rendered by ESV as "tainted," by NASB as "an offense." The Hebrew pigul (occurring only four times in the OT: Lev 7:18; 19:7; Isa 65:4; Ezek 4:14) connotes something that has become putrid, ritually invalid, morally unacceptable. "Offensive thing" preserves the sense of something that has become repugnant to YHWH.

**6. "Right to its edge" / "gleanings" (vv. 9–10) — pe'ah / leket:**
The gleaning laws of vv. 9–10 establish one of the Torah's most fundamental provisions for the poor. Two Hebrew agricultural terms anchor the laws:

- pe'ah (corner, edge, side) — v. 9: "you shall not reap your field right to its edge." The law requires leaving the "corner" or edge-strip of the field unharvested, for the poor to gather.
- leket (from laqat, "to gather, pick up") — v. 9: "the gleanings of your harvest." Fallen or missed sheaves that have been dropped during harvest are leket — left for the poor.
- The same pair appears in v. 10 applied to the vineyard: do not strip it bare (lo te'olel — from alal, "to glean completely, strip"), nor gather the fallen grapes (peret — individual fallen grapes). These are to be left "for the poor and for the sojourner."

The rendering "reap your field right to its edge" (rather than "reap to the corners" or "harvest the corner") captures pe'ah as the extremity of the field. The gleaning laws are the backdrop for the Book of Ruth (cf. Ruth 2:2–23), where Ruth the Moabitess gathers leket in Boaz's field.

**7. "Deal falsely" / "lie" (v. 11) — lo teshaqru / lo tekahashu:**
Verse 11 contains three staccato prohibitions: lo tignovu ("you shall not steal"), lo teshaqru ("you shall not deal falsely" or "you shall not act deceptively"), lo tekahashu ("you shall not lie" — from kachash, "to deny, lie, act deceptively"). The three terms form an ascending triad moving from taking (theft), to acting (deception), to speaking (lying). The verb shaqar (Pi'el, "to deal falsely, deceive") and kachash ("to lie, deny") are related but distinct: shaqar emphasizes false dealing in conduct, kachash emphasizes false speech and denial. "Deal falsely" for shaqar (NASB) and "lie" for kachash are the standard formal-equivalent renderings.

**8. "Slanderer" (v. 16) — rakhil:**
The Hebrew term rakhil (from rakal, "to go about, trade, traffic") in the phrase lo telekh rakhil ("you shall not go about as a slanderer") denotes a tale-bearer, gossip, one who traffics in information harmful to others. The rendering "slanderer" is chosen over "gossip" (which is too light) and "tale-bearer" (KJV, more archaic). The same root appears in Jer 6:28 and Ezek 22:9. The prohibition is immediately followed by "you shall not stand against the blood of your neighbor" (lo ta'amod al dam re'akha) — a prohibition on bearing witness or taking action that endangers someone's life. The phrase "stand against the blood" is the formal rendering of the Hebrew idiom; it may refer to malicious testimony in capital cases, or more broadly to any action that puts a neighbor's life at risk.

**9. "Surely reprove" (v. 17) — hokhiach tokhiach:**
The infinitive absolute construction hokhiach tokhiach (Hiphil infinitive absolute + Hiphil imperfect of yakach, "to reprove, rebuke, correct") is a classic Hebrew intensive construction: the doubling of the verb intensifies it. The rendering "you shall surely reprove your neighbor" captures the emphatic force: reproof is not optional. The verb yakach (Hiphil) means to argue, reprove, correct, convict — the most common rendering is "rebuke" (NIV) or "reprove" (ESV, NASB). "Reprove" is preferred as more appropriately formal. The verse frames fraternal reproof as the alternative to harboring secret hatred (v. 17a: "you shall not hate your brother in your heart") — open correction is the loving alternative to nursing a grievance. The phrase "lest you incur sin because of him" (ve-lo tissa alav chet) presents silence in the face of a neighbor's wrong as itself potentially sinful.

**10. "Love your neighbor as yourself" (v. 18) — ve-ahavta le-re'akha kamokha:**
Verse 18 is one of the most consequential sentences in all of Scripture: ve-ahavta le-re'akha kamokha, ani YHWH — "You shall love your neighbor as yourself. I am the LORD." The verb ahav (Qal, "to love") with the preposition le ("to, for") and the reflexive/comparative kamokha ("as yourself, like yourself") creates the love-command in its foundational form. Jesus identifies this verse, together with Deut 6:4–5 (love of God), as the two commands on which "all the Law and the Prophets hang" (Matt 22:37–40; Mark 12:28–34). Paul calls it "the fulfillment of the law" (Rom 13:8–10; Gal 5:14). James calls it "the royal law" (Jas 2:8).

The rendering "you shall love your neighbor as yourself" is standard in all English translations (ESV, NASB, KJV, NIV). The word re'a ("neighbor, fellow, companion, associate") in context refers to fellow Israelites and, in the chapter's logic, extends to the sojourner (v. 34: "you shall love him as yourself"). The Septuagintal rendering (ἀγαπήσεις τὸν πλησίον σου ὡς σεαυτόν) uses ἀγαπάω (the NT love-word) and ὁ πλησίον ("the near one, neighbor"), the form Jesus uses in his teaching. The formula ani YHWH at the end of v. 18 grounds this command: love of neighbor is not a humanistic ideal but a covenantal imperative anchored in the LORD's own character.

**11. "Forbidden" / "uncircumcised" (v. 23) — arelim:**
The orla / arelim terminology in vv. 23–25 is one of the most distinctive in the chapter. Verse 23: "you shall count its fruit as forbidden" — the Hebrew is va-araltem orlato ("and you shall regard as uncircumcised its uncircumcision/foreskin"). The verb aral (to regard as uncircumcised, to treat as foreskinned) applied to a fruit-tree is a striking extension of the circumcision metaphor into agricultural law. The "foreskin" of the tree (orlato — its uncircumcised/impure fruit) is what is forbidden in years one through three. The law is typically called the "orlah law." The formal rendering "you shall count its fruit as forbidden" (following ESV) captures the practical meaning while the accompanying note preserves the literal sense. The term orla in this agricultural context denotes the firstfruits that are not yet consecrated/purified — the tree, like a male infant, passes through a period of being "uncircumcised" (unripe for consecration) before it becomes fit for offering. The fourth-year fruit is "holy, an offering of praise to the LORD" (v. 24) — tehillim la-YHWH, "praises/jubilations to the LORD." In the fifth year, the yield is for the owner's use (v. 25).

**12. "Interpret omens / tell fortunes" (v. 26) — lo tenachhashu / lo te'onenu:**
Two divinatory practices are prohibited in v. 26:
- nachash (Pi'el, "to practice divination by omens, to interpret signs") — rendered "interpret omens." The root may be related to nachash ("serpent") or to a verb meaning "to hiss/observe." It refers to reading natural signs for guidance.
- onen (Pi'el of anah or from the root 'anan, "cloud") — rendered "tell fortunes." It likely refers to cloud-reading, astrological timing (choosing auspicious times), or some form of prognostication by observation of times and patterns. The LXX renders both with οἰωνίζεσθαι ("augury, reading of bird-omens") and κληδονίζεσθαι ("reading of omens in chance utterances"), respectively.

**13. "Round off the hair on your temples" / "mar the edges of your beard" (v. 27) — lo taqifu pe'at roshem / lo tashchit et pe'at zeqanekha:**
Verse 27 contains two closely related prohibitions governing cutting practices:
- lo taqifu pe'at roshem — "you shall not round off the hair on your temples" (lit. "you shall not go around [cutting] the corner/edge of your head"). The verb naqaf (Hiphil, "to go around, surround, cut around") with pe'ah ("corner, edge, side") of the head. The prohibition is against completely rounding the hair at the sides of the head — a practice attested among certain ancient Near Eastern peoples.
- lo tashchit et pe'at zeqanekha — "you shall not mar the edges of your beard" (lit. "you shall not destroy the corner/edge of your beard"). The verb shachath (Hiphil, "to destroy, ruin, corrupt") is strikingly strong — "mar" (ESV) is the standard rendering. Pe'ah of the beard refers to the sides and corners of the beard.
These prohibitions are the basis for the Jewish practice of payot (the uncut side-curls worn by observant Jewish men). The exact scope of the prohibition is defined differently in later halakhic tradition.

**14. "Cuts on your body for the dead" (v. 28) — lo tittenu qeritot ba-besarkhem la-nafesh:**
The prohibition in v. 28 encompasses two practices: la-nafesh ("for the dead/soul" — la-nafesh, lit. "for a soul/person," referring to a deceased person; the same usage as in Num 6:6 and 9:6), qeritot (incisions, cuts — from qarat, "to cut"), and the tattoo prohibition — akhtovet qa'aqa ("written inscription, tattoo mark"). The rendering "cuts on your body for the dead" preserves the literal sense of qeritot ba-besarkhem ("incisions/cuts in your flesh") in the context of mourning rituals. The practice of self-laceration in mourning is attested in the ancient Near East and is prohibited elsewhere (Deut 14:1). The word qa'aqa (hapax legomenon in the OT) is clearly a tattoo or permanent skin-mark; "tattoo yourselves" is the standard rendering.

**15. "Depravity" (v. 29) — zimmah:**
The term zimmah reappears here (as in 18:17) in a different context: making a daughter a prostitute causes "the land to fall into prostitution and the land become full of depravity" (u-male'ah ha-aretz zimmah). The social/communal consequence of exploiting one's daughter sexually is the pollution of the entire land. The land-contamination language of chapter 18 resurfaces: immoral sexual practices are not merely private sins but cosmic-moral pollutants that corrupt the social and even geographical fabric.

**16. "Mediums" / "necromancers" (v. 31) — ha-ovot / ha-yidde'onim:**
Two classes of practitioners are prohibited:
- ov (plural ovot) — "medium," one who consults the "ob" spirit, typically identified with a chthonic spirit of the dead contacted through a medium (cf. the woman at Endor, 1 Sam 28). The LXX renders ovot with ἐγγαστρίμυθοι ("ventriloquists, those who speak from the belly"), reflecting a tradition that the ov spirit was ventriloquized through the medium.
- yidde'oni (from yada, "to know") — "necromancer, familiar spirit, one who knows" — a practitioner who claims special knowledge from the dead. Rendered "necromancers" following ESV/NASB tradition.

**17. "Just balances... ephah... hin" (v. 36) — even tsedek / eifah tsedek / hin tsedek:**
The concluding commercial-justice legislation in vv. 35–36 specifies four instruments of fair dealing: moznei tsedek (just balances/scales), avnei tsedek (just weights/stones), eifat tsedek (a just ephah), hin tsedek (a just hin). The ephah (eifah) is a dry measure (approximately 22 liters / 0.6 bushel); the hin (hin) is a liquid measure (approximately 3.6–4 liters). Both terms are preserved as transliterations following ESV/NASB convention. The word tsedek ("justice, righteousness") applied to measuring instruments creates an important theological point: justice is not merely a legal or relational category — it extends to commercial instruments, to the accuracy of every economic transaction. The passage closes with the Exodus formula: "I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt" — grounding the commercial ethics in the redemptive history of YHWH, who paid Israel's full debt and brought them out of bondage.

---

### LXX Vaticanus Variants

| Verse | MT (Hebrew) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Note |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|------|
| 2 | ki qadosh ani YHWH eloheikhem ("for I the LORD your God am holy") | ὅτι ἐγὼ ἅγιός εἰμι κύριος ὁ θεὸς ὑμῶν | LXX expands slightly: "for I am holy — the LORD your God"; same content, slightly different word order and expansion |
| 9 | pe'at sadekha ("the edge of your field") | τὸ ἄκρον τοῦ ἀγροῦ σου | LXX: ἄκρον = "extremity, tip, corner"; exact equivalent of pe'ah |
| 13 | lo ta'ashok re'akha ("you shall not oppress your neighbor") | οὐκ ἀδικήσεις τὸν πλησίον σου | LXX: ἀδικέω = "do injustice to"; same sense; πλησίον for re'a as in v. 18 |
| 14 | ve-yare'ta me-Elohekha ("you shall fear your God") | καὶ φοβηθήσῃ κύριον τὸν θεόν σου | LXX adds κύριον ("the LORD") before τὸν θεόν σου — "you shall fear the LORD your God"; MT has simply "your God" |
| 16 | lo ta'amod al dam re'akha ("you shall not stand against the blood of your neighbor") | οὐκ ἐπιστήσῃ ἐπὶ αἷμα τοῦ πλησίον σου | LXX: exact structural equivalent; ἐπιστήσῃ = "stand upon/against" |
| 17 | hokhiach tokhiach et amitekha ("you shall surely reprove your neighbor") | ἐλεγμῷ ἐλέγξεις τὸν πλησίον σου | LXX preserves the Hebrew infinitive absolute with cognate dative: ἐλεγμῷ ἐλέγξεις = "with a reproof you shall reprove"; elegant formal equivalence |
| 18 | ve-ahavta le-re'akha kamokha ("you shall love your neighbor as yourself") | ἀγαπήσεις τὸν πλησίον σου ὡς σεαυτόν | LXX: ἀγαπάω (love) + πλησίον (neighbor) + ὡς σεαυτόν (as yourself); the exact form cited in the NT (Matt 22:39; Rom 13:9; Gal 5:14; Jas 2:8) |
| 20 | ve-lo yumetu ki lo chupasha ("they shall not be put to death, because she was not free") | καὶ οὐκ ἀποθανοῦνται, ὅτι οὐκ ἀπηλευθερώθη | LXX: ἀπηλευθερώθη = "was not set free/emancipated"; close structural equivalent |
| 23 | va-araltem orlato ("you shall count it as uncircumcised") | καὶ περικαθαριεῖτε τὴν ἀκαθαρσίαν αὐτοῦ | LXX: "you shall purify away its impurity"; renders orla-concept with ἀκαθαρσία ("impurity") and purification verb rather than the circumcision idiom; significant interpretive shift — the LXX desexualizes the agricultural metaphor |
| 24 | tehillim la-YHWH ("an offering of praise to the LORD") | ἀγίασμα αἰνέσεως τῷ κυρίῳ | LXX: "a consecration of praise to the LORD"; ἀγίασμα ("consecrated thing/sanctification") + αἴνεσις ("praise"); close but adds the consecration dimension |
| 26 | lo tenachhashu / lo te'onenu ("you shall not interpret omens / you shall not tell fortunes") | οὐκ οἰωνιεῖσθε / οὐδὲ κληδονιεῖσθε | LXX: οἰωνίζομαι (read bird-omens, augury) / κληδονίζομαι (read chance-omens, observe portents); functional equivalents for the two divinatory practices |
| 27 | pe'at roshem / pe'at zeqanekha ("the edge of your head / the edge of your beard") | τὴν ὄψιν τῆς κεφαλῆς ὑμῶν / τὴν ὄψιν τοῦ πώγωνός σου | LXX: ὄψις = "appearance, look" rather than "edge/corner"; the rendering shifts from the geometrical corner-idiom to the visual appearance — "you shall not disfigure the appearance of your head/beard" |
| 28 | qa'aqa ("tattoo") | γράμματα στικτά ("pricked/punctured letters/marks") | LXX: a precise technical rendering — γράμματα ("letters, marks") + στικτά ("punctured, pricked in") — "pricked-in marks"; the most literal Greek equivalent for tattooing |
| 31 | ha-ovot ve-el ha-yidde'onim ("the mediums and the necromancers") | τοῖς ἐγγαστριμύθοις / τοῖς τερατοσκόποις | LXX: ἐγγαστρίμυθος ("ventriloquist/belly-speaker") for ov; τερατοσκόπος ("observer of prodigies/diviner") for yidde'oni; significant: LXX renders yidde'oni as "diviner" rather than "familiar spirit/ghost-knower" |
| 34 | ve-ahavta lo kamokha ("you shall love him as yourself") | ἀγαπήσεις αὐτὸν ὡς σεαυτόν | LXX: exact parallel with v. 18 love-command; the explicit extension of neighbor-love to the sojourner is structurally preserved |
| 36 | eifat tsedek ve-hin tsedek ("a just ephah and a just hin") | ζυγοὶ δίκαιοι καὶ σταθμία δίκαια καὶ χοῦς δίκαιος | LXX restructures and partially harmonizes; χοῦς (dry measure) for eifah; the hin equivalent varies in LXX tradition |

---

### Consistent Term Renderings

| Hebrew Term | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|-------------|-----------------|-------|
| YHWH | the LORD | Small capitals; conventional throughout |
| ani YHWH | I am the LORD | Self-identification formula; 15 occurrences in ch. 19 |
| ani YHWH eloheikhem | I am the LORD your God | Full form at vv. 2, 3, 4, 10, 25, 31, 34, 36 |
| kol eda | entire congregation | Full assembly of Israel; unusual address for Levitical legislation |
| qadosh / qedoshim | holy | The grounding character; vv. 2 (three times in various forms) |
| tira'u | revere | v. 3: parents; stronger than "honor" (kaved); yare root |
| elilim | idols | Contemptuous term: "nothings, worthless things" |
| massekhot | cast images | Molten/poured metal images |
| zevach shelamim | peace offerings | Fellowship/well-being offering; communal meal |
| pigul | offensive thing | v. 7: ritually invalid third-day sacrifice |
| pe'ah | edge | Corner/edge of field (gleaning law); lit. "side" |
| leket | gleanings | Dropped/fallen harvest; left for the poor |
| re'a | neighbor | vv. 13, 15, 16, 17, 18; extended to sojourner in v. 34 |
| ger | sojourner | vv. 10, 33, 34; non-Israelite resident |
| rakhil | slanderer | One who traffics in harmful information |
| ahav | love | ve-ahavta: the great love command; vv. 18, 34 |
| kamokha | as yourself | Reflexive comparative in the love command |
| orla / arelim | forbidden / uncircumcised | Agricultural extension of circumcision metaphor; vv. 23–25 |
| ov / yidde'oni | medium / necromancer | Prohibited divinatory practitioners; v. 31 |
| pe'at roshem | temples | Side/edge of the head; prohibition on rounding |
| qa'aqa | tattoo | Hapax legomenon; permanent skin-marks |
| zimmah | depravity | v. 29; same term as Lev 18:17; deliberate moral corruption |
| eifah | ephah | Preserved as transliteration; standard dry measure |
| hin | hin | Preserved as transliteration; standard liquid measure |
| tsedek | just | "Just" balances, weights, measures; commercial-legal justice |
| nachash (Pi'el) | interpret omens | Divination by natural signs |
| anan (Pi'el) | tell fortunes | Divination by times/clouds |
| bney Yisra'el | sons of Israel | Formal throughout |
| ha-ger ha-gar | the sojourner who sojourns | Cognate verbal construction; v. 33 |
| ha-ezrach | the native | v. 34: native-born Israelite; paired with sojourner |

---

— LOGOS
